title: Concurrent writers can clobber canonical tx-history JSONL because TxStorageImpl is full-file read-modify-write without lock or compare-and-swap
protected_asset: canonical wallet tx-history integrity and availability
trust_boundary: authenticated wallet RPC and backup/restore requests -> file-backed canonical JSONL store
attacker_capability_model: an authenticated wallet user or operator can issue overlapping RPC requests against the same wallet; no filesystem shell or code execution is required
main_vulnerability: TxStorageImpl loads the whole tx-history JSONL file into memory, mutates a snapshot, and rewrites the file. Atomic rename prevents torn writes, but there is no lock, version check, or compare-and-swap around the read-modify-write cycle, so overlapping writers can silently overwrite each other and lose transaction state.
implementation_nuance: Atomic replacement protects individual writes, but it does not serialize the full read-modify-write cycle. Backup export and restore also touch the same canonical JSONL path, which widens the race window.

evidence.summary: load_records() reads the entire canonical JSONL file into memory before mutation
persist_records() rewrites the entire file from the in-memory snapshot
backup RPC is backed by a shared Arc<WalletService>
dispatcher handlers clone shared RPC state into async request futures
backup export reads the same live JSONL store
restore writes imported JSONL bytes back to the canonical live path

pros: production-code evidence exists in wallet, utils, and RPC surfaces
the candidate crosses a real trust boundary through shared async RPC requests against one canonical file-backed store
backup export and restore share the same live file as tx mutation paths
cons: the attacker must achieve overlapping operations on the same wallet, so timing matters
this is an integrity and availability break, not a direct unauthorized spend or code-execution path

verification.reason: backed by production code, reachable through shared async RPC handlers, and not already neutralized by a lock, version check, or compare-and-swap

defenses: add a per-wallet lock, versioned compare-and-swap, or transactional coordinator around the tx-history read-modify-write cycle
ensure backup export and restore participate in the same single-writer contract as tx mutation paths
add a regression test that overlapping tx-history writes, or write-plus-backup interleavings, cannot silently lose a record or roll back status
residual_risk: Atomic file replacement still leaves last-writer-wins behavior unless the store gets coordination or versioning. Without that hardening, a stale snapshot can still win under concurrent access.
